items.question_text: Before an arriving aircraft crosses the landing threshold on the same runway, what is the basic rule if the preceding aircraft was also arriving?

choices.text: The preceding aircraft must have landed and be clear of the runway unless a listed daylight landmark-distance exception applies.
The preceding aircraft only needs to be past midfield on the rollout.
The preceding aircraft may still be on the runway if the arriving pilot accepts wake turbulence.
The preceding aircraft only needs to acknowledge a hold-short instruction from ground control.

items.explanation: The basic same-runway arrival rule is that the other aircraft has landed and is clear of the runway, unless one of the listed daytime landmark-distance exceptions applies.

items.instruction: Is this same-runway distance rule correct?
items.question_text: If it is between sunrise and sunset, you can determine distance by suitable landmarks, and a Category I aircraft is landing behind a landed Category I or II aircraft, the other aircraft need not be clear of the runway when at least 3,000 feet exists from the landing threshold.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the daytime landmark-distance minimum for a Category I arrival behind a landed Category I or II aircraft.

items.instruction: Is this Category III same-runway distance rule correct?
items.question_text: If the preceding departure is airborne and either aircraft is Category III, at least 6,000 feet from the landing threshold must exist before the arrival may cross the threshold.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: When either aircraft is Category III in the departure-arrival case, the minimum distance is 6,000 feet from the landing threshold.

items.question_text: This visual-separation option applies when the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph allows visual separation in lieu of the distance minima when the succeeding aircraft is a helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

items.question_text: Issue wake turbulence advisories, and the position, altitude if known, and direction of flight of departing or arriving aircraft on the same runway or parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet to the listed following aircraft categories.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the wake-advisory requirement in the paragraph.

items.instruction: Which aircraft must receive the same-runway or close-parallel wake advisory under this paragraph?
items.question_text: Which following aircraft is on the wake-advisory list?

choices.text: A Category I aircraft landing behind a Category E aircraft.
A Category A aircraft landing behind a Category I aircraft.
Any aircraft landing behind any other aircraft regardless of category.
Only IFR aircraft landing behind turbojet aircraft.

items.explanation: A Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft is one of the listed wake-advisory cases.

items.situation: A Category II arrival is on short final behind a preceding Category II departure on the same runway. The departure is airborne but only 4,000 feet from the landing threshold. Another controller says the arrival can continue because the departure is already off the pavement and no wake advisory is needed if the pilot is comfortable.
items.para_context: For a Category II arrival behind a Category I or II departure that is airborne, at least 4,500 feet from the landing threshold is required. Wake advisories are still required for the listed following categories.

choices.text: Do not let the arrival cross the threshold yet because 4,500 feet is required, and issue the required wake advisory if the category combination calls for it.
Allow the arrival to continue because airborne status automatically replaces the 4,500-foot requirement.
Allow the arrival to continue if the pilot reports the departure in sight, because same-runway distance minima no longer apply once the departure is airborne.
Clear the arrival to land and withhold any wake advisory unless the departure was Category III.

items.explanation: A Category II arrival behind a Category I or II departure requires 4,500 feet from the landing threshold before the arrival can cross it.

items.question_text: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways during arrival operations?

choices.text: Traffic information.
A go-around instruction before each landing clearance.
A hold-short instruction whether or not LAHSO is used.
A runway-change clearance before every arrival crosses the threshold.

items.explanation: Traffic information must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways.

items.instruction: Is this intersecting-runway arrival rule correct?
items.question_text: If the preceding departure has passed the intersection or flight path, or is airborne and turning to avert any conflict, the arriving aircraft may cross the landing threshold or flight path.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is one of the listed conditions that allows the arrival to continue on the intersecting runway or flight path.

items.instruction: Which preceding-arrival condition allows the intersecting-runway arrival to continue?
items.question_text: Which condition allows the arrival to continue?

choices.text: The preceding arriving aircraft completed landing roll and will hold short of the intersection or flight path.
The preceding arriving aircraft is about to touch down and is expected to stop before the intersection.
The preceding arriving aircraft says it can exit quickly after rollout.
The preceding arriving aircraft is on short final and the tower intends to issue a late hold-short instruction.

items.explanation: One listed protection is a preceding arrival that completed landing roll and will hold short of the intersection or flight path.

items.instruction: Is this LAHSO acknowledgment requirement correct?
items.question_text: When using land and hold short procedures for intersecting-runway operations, you must issue traffic information to both aircraft involved and obtain an acknowledgment from each.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Both aircraft must receive traffic information and both must acknowledge it in the LAHSO case.

items.instruction: Is this crossing-runway wake interval correct?
items.question_text: If an arrival will fly through the airborne path of a departing Category A aircraft on a crossing runway, Category B through I aircraft require 3 minutes unless radar separation is used.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the listed interval for Category B through I behind Category A in the crossing-runway arrival-behind-departure case.

items.instruction: Is this crossing-runway wake-advisory directive correct?
items.question_text: Issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories, the position, altitude if known, and direction of flight of Category A, B, C, D, or E aircraft to the listed arrivals when the arrival flight path will cross.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the wake-advisory requirement for the listed crossing-runway arrival cases.

items.situation: You plan to use LAHSO for an arriving aircraft on one runway while another aircraft uses the intersecting runway. The hold-short instruction has been prepared, but another controller says you can wait until after landing clearance to give the traffic information and get acknowledgments because both pilots can see the runways. No operational benefit will be lost if the lesser-weight aircraft is restricted instead.
items.para_context: For LAHSO in this paragraph, traffic information must be issued to both aircraft and acknowledgments obtained from each. The conditions in subparagraphs b1 and b2 must be met in sufficient time to take other action if desired by the pilots and no later than the time landing clearance is issued.

choices.text: Issue the traffic information and obtain both acknowledgments in sufficient time and no later than landing clearance, and if no operational benefit is lost, restrict the lesser-weight aircraft.
Wait until after landing clearance to give the traffic information because visual acquisition by both pilots replaces the acknowledgment requirement.
Use LAHSO without getting both acknowledgments if the arriving aircraft accepts the hold-short instruction.
Always restrict the heavier aircraft in simultaneous landings because wake concerns override the paragraph's weight guidance.

items.explanation: The LAHSO conditions must be satisfied in time for the pilots to act and no later than landing clearance, and both aircraft must receive traffic information and acknowledge it.

items.question_text: Under the basic same-runway arrival rule, what must normally exist before the arrival crosses the landing threshold if the preceding aircraft was also arriving?
items.explanation: Normally the preceding arrival must have landed and be clear of the runway unless a listed landmark-distance exception applies.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft has landed and is clear of the runway, unless a listed exception applies.
The preceding aircraft is past the touchdown zone.
The pilot accepts wake turbulence.
The preceding aircraft has reported braking action.

items.question_text: Between sunrise and sunset, what minimum threshold distance allows a Category I arrival to land behind a landed Category I or II aircraft when suitable landmark references are available?
items.explanation: The Category I minimum in that case is 3,000 feet.

choices.text: 3,000 feet.
4,500 feet.
6,000 feet.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: Between sunrise and sunset, what minimum threshold distance allows a Category II arrival to land behind a landed Category I or II aircraft when suitable landmark references are available?
items.explanation: The Category II minimum in that case is 4,500 feet.

choices.text: 4,500 feet.
3,000 feet.
6,000 feet.
2,500 feet.

items.question_text: When the preceding departure is airborne, what minimum threshold distance applies if either aircraft is Category III?
items.explanation: If either aircraft is Category III, the minimum is 6,000 feet.

choices.text: 6,000 feet.
4,500 feet.
3,000 feet.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: Which succeeding aircraft may use visual separation instead of the same-runway distance minima under this paragraph?
items.explanation: The exception applies to a succeeding helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.

choices.text: A helicopter or powered-lift aircraft.
Any Category II aircraft.
Any aircraft landing behind a Category III departure.
Only military aircraft.

items.question_text: Which following aircraft is specifically included in the same-runway wake-advisory list?
items.explanation: A Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft is one of the listed cases.

choices.text: A Category I aircraft landing behind a Category E aircraft.
A Category A aircraft landing behind a Category I aircraft.
Only aircraft behind Category III traffic.
Only arrivals behind departures on crossing runways.

items.question_text: What must be issued to each aircraft operating on intersecting runways during arrival operations?
items.explanation: Traffic information is required for each aircraft operating on intersecting runways.

choices.text: Traffic information.
A go-around instruction before each landing clearance.
A mandatory hold-short instruction in every case.
A missed-approach briefing before crossing the threshold.

items.question_text: Which preceding-departure condition allows an arrival to cross the landing threshold or intersecting flight path under this paragraph?
items.explanation: One listed condition is that the preceding departure has passed the intersection or flight path or is airborne and turning to avert conflict.

choices.text: The preceding departure has passed the intersection or flight path, or is airborne and turning to avert any conflict.
The preceding departure has rotated but is still tracking directly through the conflict area.
The preceding departure is in line up and wait on the other runway.
The preceding departure says it can expedite climb.

items.question_text: Which preceding-arrival condition allows an arrival to continue on an intersecting runway or intersecting flight path?
items.explanation: A preceding arrival that completed landing roll and will hold short of the intersection or flight path is one of the listed protections.

choices.text: The preceding arrival completed landing roll and will hold short of the intersection or flight path.
The preceding arrival is touching down and expected to stop before the intersection.
The preceding arrival reports the intersection in sight.
The preceding arrival has been switched to ground frequency.

items.question_text: When using LAHSO under this paragraph, what must be done for both aircraft involved?
items.explanation: Traffic information must be issued to both aircraft and an acknowledgment obtained from each.

choices.text: Issue traffic information to both aircraft and obtain an acknowledgment from each.
Issue traffic information only to the aircraft that will hold short.
Obtain acknowledgment only from the departing aircraft because it has the shorter reaction window.
Wait until after landing clearance to get any acknowledgment as long as both pilots see the airport layout.

items.question_text: No later than when must the LAHSO conditions in subparagraphs b1 and b2 be met?
items.explanation: They must be met in sufficient time for other action if desired and no later than landing clearance.

choices.text: No later than the time landing clearance is issued.
No later than rollout completion.
No later than the aircraft's base-to-final turn.
No later than tower handoff from approach control.

items.question_text: If an arrival will fly through the airborne path of a departing Category A aircraft on a crossing runway, what interval applies to Category B through I arrivals unless radar separation is used?
items.explanation: The listed interval is 3 minutes.

choices.text: 3 minutes.
2 minutes.
4 minutes.
90 seconds.
